text:
Invasive fungal infections (IFIs) have become a major global health challenge. Many patients have increased susceptibility to these infections, such as patients with hematological malignancies, solid tumors and diabetes, etc. Rapid and accurate diagnosis is key to managing such infections, as early containment of an IFI can prevent progression to severe, often lethal forms. Even though conventional microscopy and culture remain as backbone for the diagnosis of mycological infections, many new sophisticated diagnostic tools have come into play and have already started to dominate.

Fungal diagnostics – a dynamic update This review is designed to be a methodical and comprehensive update on developments in the rapidly moving field of fungal diagnostics. Over the past decade or so, traditional culture-dependent methodologies have been gradually replaced with non-culture-based methods, and an increasing number of new PCR techniques have been introduced and validated in the clinic. In addition, emerging technologies including the application of microfluidic chips, next-generation sequencing, nanotechnology-based biosensors and artificial intelligence for development of predictive models are being investigated and explored in the context of IFI. This review appears to be the first integrated summary of these developing and promising adjuncts, particularly where biosensor technology is linked with machine learning, an area not previously reviewed in the context of IFI. A valuable tool for those involved in IFI diagnostics and research, this review bridges the gap between traditional methods and contemporary molecular technologies.
explanation: The text is coherent and reads like a polished abstract with broad, generic claims and no study specifics; such features are common in AI-generated writing but can also appear in human reviews.
